title: Responses to the environment
description: Test your knowledge of responses to the environment!

assessment_items.question_content:
In an experiment about seed germination, a student placed 30 lettuce seeds on growth medium in each of 5 petri dishes. The student then exposed each petri dish to a different pattern of light and dark, as shown in Figure 1.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Figure 1:** Seed germination experiment. Seeds were exposed to darkness (gray bars), flashes of red light (light red squares), and/or flashes of far-red light (dark red squares). Each flash of light lasted one minute.

After seven days, the student calculated the percentage of seeds on each petri dish that germinated. The results are summarized in the following table.

Treatment | Percentage germinated seeds
:-: | :-:
Dark control | $8.5\%$
Treatment 1 | $98\%$
Treatment 2 | $43\%$ 
Treatment 3 | $100\%$
Treatment 4 | $54\%$

**Which of the following observations is best supported by the information provided above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Five horizontal bars parallel to each other. The first bar is labeled Dark control and is a solid gray bar. The other 4 bars are labeled as treatments and have the following patterns: Treatment 1: gray bar, bright red box, gray bar. Treatment 2: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, gray bar. Treatment 3: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, bright red box, gray bar. Treatment 4: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, bright red box, dark red box, gray bar.

choices.content: More seeds germinated when the last flash of light they were exposed to was red versus far-red.
More seeds germinated when the first flash of light they were exposed to was red versus far-red.
The more flashes of light seeds were exposed to, the greater the germination rate.
The more time seeds were exposed to light, the greater the germination rate.

assessment_items.question_content:
In an experiment about seed germination, a student placed 30 lettuce seeds on growth medium in each of 5 petri dishes. The student then exposed each petri dish to a different pattern of light and dark, as shown in Figure 1.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Figure 1:** Seed germination experiment. Seeds were exposed to darkness (gray bars), flashes of red light (light red squares), and/or flashes of far-red light (dark red squares). Each flash of light lasted one minute.

After seven days, the student calculated the percentage of seeds on each petri dish that germinated. The results are summarized in the following table.

Treatment | Percentage germinated seeds
:-: | :-:
Dark control | $8.5\%$
Treatment 1 | $98\%$
Treatment 2 | $43\%$ 
Treatment 3 | $100\%$
Treatment 4 | $54\%$

**Which of the following observations is most consistent with the information provided above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Five horizontal bars parallel to each other. The first bar is labeled Dark control and is a solid gray bar. The other 4 bars are labeled as treatments and have the following patterns: Treatment 1: gray bar, bright red box, gray bar. Treatment 2: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, gray bar. Treatment 3: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, bright red box, gray bar. Treatment 4: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, bright red box, dark red box, gray bar.

choices.content: Fewer seeds germinated when the last flash of light they were exposed to was far-red compared to red.
Fewer seeds germinated when the first flash of light they were exposed to was far-red versus red.
The more flashes of light seeds were exposed to, the lower the germination rate.
The more time seeds were exposed to light, the lower the germination rate.

assessment_items.question_content:
In an experiment about seed germination, a student placed 30 lettuce seeds on growth medium in each of 5 petri dishes. The student then exposed each petri dish to a different pattern of light and dark, as shown in Figure 1.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Figure 1:** Seed germination experiment. Seeds were exposed to darkness (gray bars), flashes of red light (light red squares), and/or flashes of far-red light (dark red squares). Each flash of light lasted one minute.

After seven days, the student calculated the percentage of seeds on each petri dish that germinated. The results are summarized in the following table.

Treatment | Percentage germinated seeds
:-: | :-:
Dark control | $8.5\%$
Treatment 1 | $98\%$
Treatment 2 | $43\%$ 
Treatment 3 | $100\%$
Treatment 4 | $54\%$

**According to the information given above, the rate of lettuce seed germination was primarily determined by**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Five horizontal bars parallel to each other. The first bar is labeled Dark control and is a solid gray bar. The other 4 bars are labeled as treatments and have the following patterns: Treatment 1: gray bar, bright red box, gray bar. Treatment 2: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, gray bar. Treatment 3: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, bright red box, gray bar. Treatment 4: gray bar, bright red box, dark red box, bright red box, dark red box, gray bar.

choices.content: the color of the last flash of light seeds were exposed to.
the color of the first flash of light seeds were exposed to.
the number of flashes of light seeds were exposed to.
the total amount of time seeds were exposed to red and far-red light.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following passage:

> Male great bowerbirds (*Chlamydera nuchalis*) spend a significant amount of time and energy building structures called bowers. Bowers include a complex arrangement of sticks, rocks, and bones, along with colorful objects like flowers, leaves, and fruit. *C. nuchalis* females visit multiple bowers and choose a mate based on the male’s courtship display and bower quality.

**According to the evidence provided in the passage, which of the following best describes how bower building provides a fitness advantage to *C. nuchalis* males?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Bower building increases the chance that the male will mate and produce offspring.
Males who build bowers expend energy, which improves their long-term health.
Bowers can provide the male’s offspring with shelter from predators.
Bower building increases the chance that a female will bring food to the male during a bower visit.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following passage:

> Rattlesnakes are venomous pit vipers commonly found in North America. These snakes are named for the series of hollow segments on the tips of their tails, which are called rattles. A rattlesnake can shake its rattle up to 50 times per second, making a loud buzzing sound. The sound is used as a way to communicate to predators that the snake is venomous.

> During the spring breeding season, pairs of male rattlesnakes compete for access to females. To do this, the males raise the front half of their bodies high off the ground and then intertwine, trying to knock each other to the ground. Even though the snakes are venomous, they do not bite each other during this courtship battle.

**According to the evidence provided in the passage, which of the following best describes how rattle shaking provides a fitness advantage to rattlesnakes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Rattle shaking acts as a warning signal, decreasing the chance that a rattlesnake will be killed for food.
Rattle shaking is a courtship display, increasing the chances of a male rattlesnake attracting a mate.
Rattle shaking acts as a display of dominance for one male over other males during courtship rituals.
Rattle shaking is a territorial signal that is used to establish a snake’s hunting territory.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following passage:

> Male túngara frogs attract females through vocalizations, which can have varying levels of complexity. The most complex vocalizations require movements of a large vocal sac that create ripples in the frogs’ ponds. These ripples can be detected by predatory bats through echolocation. Despite this, many male túngara frogs make these vocalizations because females are more likely to be attracted to complex calls than simple ones.

**According to the evidence provided in the passage, which of the following best describes how the ability to make complex vocalizations provides a fitness advantage to male túngara frogs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Complex vocalizations increase the chance that the male will mate and produce offspring.
Complex vocalizations increase the chance that a nearby male competing for mates will be killed by a bat.
Complex vocalizations increase the chance that a female túngara frog will bring the male food.
Complex vocalizations increase the chance that the male will be able to protect offspring from predators.

assessment_items.question_content:
When foraging honeybees return to their hive, they communicate information about food sources through a behavior called a waggle dance. The waggle dance allows the dancer bee to communicate the direction, distance, and quality of a food source to the bees watching the dance, which are called followers. Followers can then fly efficiently to the indicated food source, bringing more food back to the colony.

**Which of the following describes the most likely evolutionary explanation for the honeybee behavior described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The waggle dance directs the behavior of the follower bees, which increases the overall fitness of the bee colony.
The waggle dance recruits foragers from other hives to bring food to the dancer bee’s colony.
The waggle dance points other bees away from a food source, ensuring the food source remains abundant for the dancer bee.
The waggle dance leads to more bees leaving the hive and starting new colonies, freeing up resources for the dancer bee’s colony.

assessment_items.question_content:
When a foraging ant returns to the colony from a food source, it emits a chemical signal called a pheromone that marks a trail for other ants to follow. The other ants can follow the pheromone trail, traveling efficiently to the indicated food source. 

When the food source is plentiful, all of the ants returning to the colony emit the pheromone, reinforcing the signal. When the food source is depleted, the returning ants stop emitting the pheromone, and the trail eventually goes away.

**Which of the following describes the most likely evolutionary explanation for the ant behavior described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The pheromone emitted by one ant directs the behavior of other ants, which increases the overall fitness of the colony.
The pheromone recruits ants from other colonies to bring food to the colony of the ant that originally emitted the pheromone.
The pheromone trail points other ants away from a food source, ensuring the food source remains abundant for the ant that originally emitted the pheromone.
The pheromone trail causes more ants to leave and start new colonies, freeing up resources for the ant that originally emitted the pheromone.

assessment_items.question_content:
When a foraging french termite (*Reticulitermes santonensis*) finds a food source, it quickly returns to its colony, dragging its abdomen on the ground along the way. This dragging behavior allows the termite to lay down a pheromone trail that other termites in the colony can follow to reach the food source.

**Which of the following describes the most likely evolutionary explanation for the termite behavior described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The pheromone emitted by one termite directs the behavior of other termites, which increases the overall fitness of the colony.
The pheromone recruits termites from other colonies to bring food to the colony of the termite that originally emitted the pheromone.
The pheromone trail points other termites away from a food source, ensuring the food source remains abundant for the termite that originally emitted the pheromone.
The pheromone trail causes more termites to leave and start new colonies, freeing up resources for the termite that originally emitted the pheromone.

assessment_items.question_content:
When a minnow is injured by a predatory fish, it emits a chemical alarm substance that can be detected by nearby minnows. To test the relationship between this type of alarm substance and behavior in minnows, a group of scientists carried out an experiment in the laboratory. 

First, the scientists exposed minnows in one tank to plain water, and minnows in another tank to water containing a chemical alarm substance. Then, the scientists measured the minnows’ schooling (gathering) behavior, and their sheltering (hiding) behavior (Figures 1 and 2, respectively).

[[☃ image 1]]
**Figure 1:** Effect of alarm substance on minnow schooling behavior. A higher schooling index indicates more schooling.

[[☃ image 2]]
**Figure 2:** Effect of alarm substance on minnow sheltering behavior. A higher sheltering index indicates more sheltering.

Next, the scientists tested if the minnows’ response to an alarm substance would help protect them from a predator. To do this, the scientists placed minnows and a predatory fish called a pike together in each of two tanks. They added plain water to one of the tanks and water with a chemical alarm substance to the other, and measured the amount of time before a minnow was captured by the pike in each tank. The results are shown in Figure 3.

[[☃ image 3]]
**Figure 3:** Average time in seconds before a pike captured a minnow in water without and with an alarm substance.

**Which of the following connections between behavior and evolutionary fitness is best supported by the experiment described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph with Schooling Index on the y axis. The data columns on the x axis are as follows: Without alarm substance, 0.09; With alarm substance, 0.92.
A bar graph with Sheltering Index on the y axis. The data columns on the x axis are as follows: Without alarm substance, 0.1; With alarm substance, 0.7.
A bar graph with Time before first capture (s) on the y axis. The data columns on the x axis are as follows: Without alarm substance, 200; With alarm substance, 300.

choices.content: Minnows that decrease sheltering and schooling behaviors in response to an alarm substance are more likely to escape predators in the wild.
Minnows that increase sheltering and schooling behaviors in response to an alarm substance are more likely to escape predators in the wild.
In the wild, pike that produce an alarm substance when preying on minnows are likely to be more reproductively successful than pike that do not.
In the wild, pike that produce an alarm substance when preying on minnows are likely to be less reproductively successful than pike that do not.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alpine accentors are birds that live in the mountainous regions of Europe and Asia. Female alpine accentors can produce complex songs that consist of repeated syllables and trills. 

A group of researchers studied the singing behavior of female alpine accentors in the French Pyrenees mountains. Using careful observations and recordings, the researchers first determined the conditions under which females were most likely to sing (Figures 1 and 2).

[[☃ image 1]]
**Figure 1.** Mean vocalizations per minute for female alpine accentors during the fertile period, the period of egg incubation (Incubation period), and the period of caring for baby birds in the nest (Nestling care period).

[[☃ image 2]]
**Figure 2.** Mean vocalizations per minute for female alpine accentors during the fertile period, either while alone or when in the presence of a male.

The researchers then recorded the responses of nearby birds to the female vocalizations (Figure 3).

[[☃ image 3]]
**Figure 3.** Percentage of responses to female vocalizations that consisted of a male approaching a female (Male approach), a male singing (Male song), or a female approaching or singing (Female approach or song).

**Which of the following connections between behavior and evolutionary fitness is best supported by the information given above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph with Mean vocalizations per minute on the y axis. The data columns on the x axis are as follows: Fertile period, 0.65 plus or minus 0.1; Incubation period, 0; Nesting care period, 0.
A bar graph with Mean vocalizations per minute on the y axis. The data columns on the x axis are as follows: Alone, 2.2 plus or minus 0.5; With one male, 0.3 plus or minus 0.1.

A bar graph with Mean percent responses to female vocalizations on the y axis. The data columns on the x axis are as follows: Male approach, 35 plus or minus 4; Male song, 5 plus or minus 2; Female approach or song, 0.

choices.content: A female alpine accentor sings in order to attract mates and increase her chances of producing offspring.
A female alpine accentor sings in order to recruit males to bring food to baby birds in the nest.
A female alpine accentor sings so that males will bring her food while she incubates her eggs in the nest.
A female alpine accentor sings as a warning to male alpine accentors to stay away from her nesting territory.

assessment_items.question_content:
African lions (*Panthera leo*) live in prides, which are social groups that work together to raise young and defend territory. Lions in a pride will occasionally hunt together to capture large prey, but they more often will hunt alone for smaller prey.

A group of scientists studied lion behavior over a 38-year period in the Serengeti plains of Tanzania. Using tracking devices and careful observations, the scientists determined that larger prides were more likely to gain disputed territory, as represented in Figure 1.

[[☃ image 1]]
**Figure 1.** Representative outcome of a territorial dispute between a large pride and a small pride. (A) In 1991-1992, the prides’ territories overlap. (B) In 1993-1994, the prides’ territories no longer overlap. The majority of the formerly disputed area is occupied by the larger pride. 

During their study, the scientists also determined that the number of individuals in a pride was correlated with the overall quality of territory held by that pride (Figure 2).

[[☃ image 4]]
**Figure 2.** Pride size and territory quality over time. Pride size is given as the number of adult females in the pride. Quality scores were assigned based on the vicinity of the territory to river confluences, which are areas that have an abundance of prey, water, and shelter.

**Which of the following connections between behavior and evolutionary fitness is best supported by the information given above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Two maps showing the same area. Several rivers run through each area. In the first map, labeled A. 1991 to 1992, an area labeled Territory of larger pride overlaps with an area labeled territory of smaller pride. The overlapping area is shaded gray, labeled Territory overlap, and contains two rivers. In the second map, labeled B. 1993 to 1994, the territory of the larger pride has increased in size and now contains most of the area labeled Formerly disputed area. The territory of smaller pride has decreased in size.

A line graph with Year on the x axis and Pride size on the y axis is overlaid on top of a line graph with Year on the x axis and Quality score on the right y-axis. The two lines roughly follow the same pattern. The data points for Pride size are as follows: 1987, 2; 1989, 2; 1991, 2.5; 1993, 4.5; 1995, 3.5; 1997, 6; 1999, 5; 2001, 5; 2003, 4.8. The data points for Territory quality are as follows: 1987, 55; 1989, 47; 1991, 60; 1993, 55; 1995, 58; 1997, 66; 1999, 68; 2001, 58; 2003, 60.

choices.content: African lions living in larger prides are more likely to obtain and defend high-quality territory.
African lions living in smaller prides are more likely to hunt in groups than those living in larger prides.
African lions living in smaller prides are more likely to migrate to new territories where food is abundant.
African lions living in larger prides are more likely to obtain members from outside the pride, increasing the pride’s genetic diversity.
